compiled_state.author: Iso Tester

comments.author: Iso Tester
comments.body: ROOT CAUSE (confirmed via joe-debug): the mkdir leg of the write lock (.ticket-write.lock.d) had no stale reclamation — unlike the kernel-reclaimed fcntl leg — so a dir orphaned by a process that died mid-commit blocked every subsequent write the full ~60s budget then failed (proven: acquire with a stale dir present times out; a write hangs then errors). FIX (branch worktree-civil-marlin-flare, commit 80fef546, PR #26): acquire() stamps the owning host:pid inside the lock dir and _acquire_mkdir reclaims ONLY a lock whose stamp proves a DEAD process on THIS host — live owner / ownerless / foreign-host are never reclaimed (3 safety guards; mutation-checked). Race-safe because the fcntl leg (acquired first) serializes Python acquirers. release() removes the stamp before rmdir. Reads were already made immune in slim-fetch-ledge (commit 9943f1e1); this covers writes. Pending PR merge before close.

comments.author: Iso Tester
comments.body:
**Verification result: PASS** — all four acceptance criteria are satisfied by the implementation.

**AC1 — Stale lock reclaimed, writes proceed promptly.**  
`src/rebar/_store/lock.py`: `_mkdir_lock_is_stale()` (lines 162-181) reads the `owner` stamp from inside `.ticket-write.lock.d/`, confirms the host matches, and returns True only when `_pid_alive(pid)` is False. `_acquire_mkdir()` (lines 197-230) calls `_reclaim_mkdir_lock()` when stale is detected, then retries immediately. The fix addresses the root bug exactly.

**AC2 — Live lock holder is never reclaimed; mutual exclusion preserved.**  
Three safety guards in `_mkdir_lock_is_stale()` (live PID → False, missing/unparseable stamp → False, foreign-host → False) ensure only provably-dead local owners are reclaimed. Unit tests `test_acquire_does_not_reclaim_live_owner`, `test_acquire_does_not_reclaim_ownerless_lock`, and `test_acquire_does_not_reclaim_foreign_host_owner` in `tests/unit/test_lock_stale_reclaim.py` all assert the lock is left intact and `LockTimeout` is raised.

**AC3 — Regression test: orphaned lock, write completes promptly.**  
Two test files cover this:
- `tests/unit/test_lock_stale_reclaim.py` — unit-level: `test_acquire_reclaims_dead_owner_mkdir_lock` seeds a dead-pid stamp and asserts `acquire()` succeeds (rather than timing out).
- `tests/interfaces/store/test_stale_lock_reclaim_write.py` — integration-level: creates a real ticket, orphans the mkdir lock with a dead-pid stamp, calls `rebar.comment()`, and asserts `elapsed < 15.0s` (previously would have blocked ~60s then failed).

**AC4 — Reclamation documented in `lock.py` alongside the dual-window rule.**  
Module docstring (lines 7-23) documents the dual-window interop rule. Lines 38-41 document the ownership-stamp mechanism for reclamation. Functions `_mkdir_lock_is_stale()` and `_reclaim_mkdir_lock()` each have detailed docstrings explaining safety invariants and the `yaw-gravel-linen` bug reference. `release()` (lines 248-266) comments explain stamp removal before `rmdir`.

**Bug resolved:** the orphaned-dir reproduction path is closed — `_acquire_mkdir` now detects the dead-owner stamp and reclaims the directory, so writes proceed promptly instead of exhausting the full ~60s budget.

compiled_state.description:
## Summary
The mkdir leg of the unified write lock (`.ticket-write.lock.d`, `src/rebar/_store/lock.py`) has **no stale-lock reclamation**. The fcntl leg (`.ticket-write.lock`) is kernel-backed and auto-released when the holder dies, but `_acquire_mkdir` only polls `os.mkdir` until the deadline — so a `.ticket-write.lock.d` orphaned by a writer/push that was SIGKILLed mid-commit persists forever and makes **every subsequent write block the full lock budget (~60s) and then fail** with `LockTimeout`.

Discovered while root-causing `slim-fetch-ledge` (rebar show empty-stdout-under-contention). That P1 was fixed on the READ side (the read-path reconverge now waits only ~2s for the lock, commit 9943f1e1), so reads are immune to a stale mkdir lock. WRITES remain affected and are tracked here separately.

## Reproduction Steps
1. In an initialized store, simulate an orphaned lock: `mkdir .tickets-tracker/.ticket-write.lock.d` (no live holder).
2. Run any write, e.g. `rebar comment <id> "x"`.
3. Observe it hangs ~60s (the write lock budget = timeout x attempts) then fails with `LockTimeout`, even though no process holds the lock.

- **Expected:** a provably-stale mkdir lock (no live owner) is reclaimed so writes proceed promptly.
- **Actual:** the orphaned dir blocks every write for the full budget, then the write fails.

## Notes on a safe fix
- The fcntl leg is the real mutual-exclusion primitive (acquired first in `acquire`). The mkdir leg is a secondary dual-window/bash-interop guard.
- Reclamation must be conservative — incorrectly removing a live lock breaks mutual exclusion and can corrupt the store. Options: record the owner PID (and host/boot id) inside the lock dir on acquire and reclaim only when that PID is provably dead; and/or gate reclamation on the fact that the caller already holds the fcntl leg (safe only once the bash write core / dual_window is fully retired — verify before relying on it).
- macOS lacks util-linux `flock(1)`, so historically bash could hold ONLY the mkdir leg there ("stiff-mop-lane" gap); confirm the bash write core is fully retired before treating "we hold fcntl" as proof the mkdir dir is stale.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A provably-stale `.ticket-write.lock.d` (no live owning process) is reclaimed so a write proceeds without waiting the full lock budget.
- [ ] A LIVE lock holder is never reclaimed — mutual exclusion is preserved (concurrency test under contention).
- [ ] A regression test: with an orphaned mkdir lock (dead/absent owner), a write completes promptly instead of hanging ~60s then failing.
- [ ] Reclamation is documented in `src/rebar/_store/lock.py` alongside the dual-window rule.
compiled_state.title: mkdir write-lock leg (.ticket-write.lock.d) has no stale-lock reclamation; orphaned dir blocks all writes
